html:
Johannes Robert Rydberg, known as Janne Rydberg, was born on November 8, 1854, in Halmstad, and died on December 28, 1919, in Lund. He was a Swedish physicist best known for his work in spectroscopy, where he made a significant contribution to the later theories of atomic structure with Rydberg's formula, which describes the relationship between spectral lines.

== Biography == Rydberg was the only child of merchant Sven Rydberg and Maria Anderson Rydberg. When he was four years old, his father died, forcing him and his mother to live on limited means. From 1864 to 1873, he attended the elementary school in Halmstad, and in his final exams, he received the highest grades in mathematics and physics. In 1873, he began studying at Lund University and earned a Bachelor of Arts degree in 1875. Four years later, in 1879, he obtained a Master of Science degree with his thesis "Constructions of conic sections in 3-point and 4-point contact." During his studies, he lived and was provided with meals at the Göteborg student society, and between 1876 and 1879, he received scholarships equivalent to an annual salary for a textile worker.

== Professional Career == Rydberg began his professional career during his studies in 1876, working as a research assistant at the physics department. He became a docent (associate professor) in mathematics in 1880 and started teaching undergraduate students, while continuing to work as a research assistant. In 1882, he was appointed docent in physics with his dissertation "Studies on frictional electricity," and shortly thereafter, he became a research assistant.

At this time, he began working on the question of why the atomic weights in Mendeleev's periodic table increased randomly, but he could not find any mathematical function to describe it. His next work focused on investigating the spectra of elements and explaining their origins. In 1885, Balmer presented an empirical formula that described the wavelengths of the observed spectral lines of hydrogen. In 1887, Rydberg published his own formula, which was the first one capable of describing the spectral lines not only for hydrogen but also for other elements. After publishing a paper on this topic in 1890, Rydberg returned to his research on the periodic system.

When a professorship in physics became available in 1897, Rydberg served as an acting professor and applied for the position of full professor. However, despite strong recommendations from experts in the field, the position was awarded to Victor Bäcklund. Nevertheless, Rydberg became an extraordinary professor and head of the department in 1901, and on January 1, 1909, at the age of 54, he finally became a full professor of physics. To supplement his income, he worked part-time as a numerical reviewer for Sparbanken in Lund from 1891 and as an actuary in Malmö from 1905.

== Family and Death == In 1913, Rydberg became seriously ill and was forced to slow down his research. In 1915, he was granted a leave of absence due to his illness. He passed away in 1919 at Lund University Hospital from a cerebral hemorrhage, and he was succeeded by his student, Manne Siegbahn. Rydberg is buried at the Northern Cemetery in Lund and left behind his wife, Lydia, née Carlsson (1856–1925), his son, Helge Rydberg (1887–1968), and his daughter, Gerda Rydberg (1891–1983). He was the grandfather of the nuclear chemist Jan Rydberg (1923–2015), the actress Vivian Rydberg (1920–2011), and the illustrator, graphic artist, and painter Madelaine Rydberg (1929–1983).

== Awards == In 1970, the impact crater Rydberg on the Moon was named after him. Since 1983, the "Rydbergs Minne" association has organized pub evenings for students and staff at the Department of Physics at Lund University. The asteroid 10506 Rydberg is named after him.

== See also == Rydberg's formula

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts, but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Janne Rydberg was a Swedish physicist who discovered an important mathematical formula that helped to understand the structure of atoms. His work on light and matter laid the foundation for our modern understanding of atomic structure.
lattlast:
Janne Rydberg, born in Halmstad in 1854, became a highly significant researcher in physics, the study of how nature works. He studied at Lund University and later became a professor there. He dedicated much of his work to understanding elements and developed a mathematical formula that helped other researchers understand the structure of atoms.

Throughout his career, he worked as both a teacher and a researcher. To improve his financial situation, he also worked part-time at banks while pursuing his scientific research. He became known for his studies of how light from different substances appears, a field known as spectroscopy.

Due to the importance of his work, he received several awards posthumously. For example, there is a crater on the Moon and an asteroid in space named after him. His research continues to serve as a foundation for our understanding of small particles in nature.
